title: Cue cuts voice-agent delay by polishing speech locally with Gemma 4
friendlyTitle: Cue uses a small local AI to make voice typing feel faster
summary: A Google DeepMind case study says desktop voice agent Cue moved its speech-polishing step to Gemma 4 E4B running locally through Ollama. On its Apple-silicon benchmark, Cue reports median latency fell from 876 to 488 milliseconds, usage rose about 30%, and marginal inference cost for that step dropped to zero.
friendlySummary: Cue now cleans up spoken text with Gemma 4 on the user’s computer. Its tests say the step became much faster and cheaper to run.
whyItMatters: This is a practical pattern for hybrid AI: use cloud speech recognition where needed, then run a small local model for fast, private formatting and correction. Product teams should reproduce the vendor-reported figures, but the architecture can improve responsiveness while keeping more text processing on the device.
friendlyWhyItMatters: Voice tools feel better when text appears quickly. Local AI can also keep more processing on the device and reduce cloud costs.
